claim_text: A linear discriminant function X = x_1 + 5.9037x_2 - 7.1299x_3 - 10.1036x_4 maximizes the ratio of the difference between specific means to the standard deviations within species for Iris setosa and Iris versicolor, resulting in a mean of 12.3345 cm for setosa, -21.4815 cm for versicolor, a difference of 33.816 cm, a single-plant variance of 11.4269 cm^2 (standard deviation of 3.3804 cm), and a standard error of 0.6761 cm for means of fifty plants.
metrics: mean of 12.3345 cm, mean of -21.4815 cm, difference of 33.816 cm, variance of 11.4269 cm^2, standard deviation of 3.3804 cm, standard error of 0.6761 cm

claim_text: Using the discriminant function, the ratio of half the difference between the species to the standard deviation is 5.0018, resulting in an estimated probability of misclassification of less than three per million (approximately 2.79 per million).
metrics: ratio of 5.0018, probability of misclassification less than three per million, frequency of 2.79 per million

claim_text: An analysis of variance of the chosen compound X partitions the total sum of squares of 29673.60 (99 degrees of freedom) into 28588.05 between species (4 degrees of freedom) and 1085.55 within species (95 degrees of freedom), meaning only 3.6583 per cent. of the variation is within species and 96.3417 per cent. is between species.
metrics: total sum of squares of 29673.60 (99 DF), between species sum of squares of 28588.05 (4 DF), within species sum of squares of 1085.55 (95 DF), 3.6583 per cent. within species, 96.3417 per cent. between species

claim_text: The regression of the species-determined variate y on the four measurements is highly significant, with a calculated z-value of 3.2183 for 4 degrees of freedom for regression and 95 degrees of freedom for residual variation, where the regression sum of squares is 24.0854 and the remainder sum of squares is 0.9146.
metrics: z-value of 3.2183, regression sum of squares of 24.0854 (4 DF), remainder sum of squares of 0.9146 (95 DF), total sum of squares of 25.0000 (99 DF)
evidence: Section IV, Section V, Table VII

claim_text: A linear compound designed to discriminate three species (I. virginica, I. versicolor, and I. setosa) yields mean values of 38.24827 (SD = 4.342) for I. virginica, 22.93888 (SD = 4.222) for I. versicolor, and -10.75042 (SD = 2.444) for I. setosa, showing a difference of 33.69 units between I. setosa and I. versicolor and 15.31 units between I. virginica and I. versicolor.
metrics: I. virginica mean of 38.24827 (SD = 4.342), I. versicolor mean of 22.93888 (SD = 4.222), I. setosa mean of -10.75042 (SD = 2.444), setosa-versicolor difference of 33.69 units, virginica-versicolor difference of 15.31 units

claim_text: A test of the hypothesis that the differences between the species means are in a 2:1 ratio (representing additive gene effects in a polyploid hybrid) shows a non-significant discrepancy of 3.07052 (or 3.071), with a sampling variance of 4.8365 and a standard error of 2.199 (or 2.231 if using an alternative degrees of freedom adjustment).
metrics: discrepancy of 3.07052 (or 3.071), sampling variance of 4.8365, standard error of 2.199 (or 2.231)
